packages.name: chalk
packages.synopsis: Package chalk is a Go port of Node's chalk — expressive terminal string styling.
packages.doc:
Package chalk is a Go port of Node's chalk — expressive terminal string
styling. Like the original JavaScript library, it wraps text in ANSI SGR
(Select Graphic Rendition) escape sequences so that terminals render it with
color, weight and other attributes. Styles are chainable and compose
naturally:

	fmt.Println(chalk.New().Red().Bold().Sprint("error!"))
	fmt.Println(chalk.Green("ok"))                 // package shortcut
	fmt.Println(chalk.New().Hex("#ff8800").Sprint("orange"))

Reach for chalk whenever a command-line program wants to highlight output —
errors in red, success in green, hints in a dim gray — without hand-writing
escape codes or pulling in a dependency. The package is standard-library only.
Every entry point comes in two flavors: a fluent [Style] built with [New] and
chained methods for reuse, and package-level shortcuts such as [Red] or [Hex]
for one-off styling. A [Style] is immutable — each method returns a new value
— so a configured style is safe to store and share across goroutines.

Internally a [Style] records a list of open/close SGR code pairs. At render
time each pair is emitted as an escape sequence of the form ESC[<codes>m
around the text, applying the innermost style first so that outer styles
re-assert themselves. When a piece of text already contains a matching close
code (from a nested style), chalk re-opens the outer style immediately after
it, mirroring the "style bleed" fix in Node chalk so nested colors survive.
Foreground and background colors, the 16 basic and 16 bright ANSI colors, the
256-color palette ([Style.Ansi256]), 24-bit truecolor ([Style.RGB]) and hex
strings ([Style.Hex]) are all supported.

Color output is enabled automatically when stdout is a terminal and NO_COLOR
is unset. Detection follows the same conventions as the Node ecosystem: the
[Level] is derived from NO_COLOR, FORCE_COLOR, COLORTERM and TERM (see
[GetLevel] and [SetLevel]). Truecolor and 256-color requests degrade
gracefully down to the nearest color the terminal actually supports, so an
[Style.RGB] call still produces reasonable output on a 16-color terminal and
emits nothing at all when the level is [LevelNone]. Detection happens once and
is cached; [ResetDetection] forces a re-detect and [SetLevel] pins a fixed
level, which is the recommended way to get deterministic output in tests.

A handful of edge cases are worth knowing. When color is disabled the render
methods return the input text unchanged, so it is always safe to wrap output
in a style. [Strip] removes SGR sequences from a string and [VisibleLength]
reports the on-screen width (counting runes, not bytes, and ignoring escape
codes), which is useful for laying out tables or padding colored columns.
Because detection keys off os.Stdout, redirecting output to a file or pipe
disables color automatically unless FORCE_COLOR overrides it.

Parity with Node chalk is close but not exact. The chainable API, automatic
level detection, the NO_COLOR/FORCE_COLOR conventions and the truecolor→256→16
downgrade all match. The differences are idiomatic: styling is applied with
Sprint/Sprintf/Println methods (and package shortcuts) rather than by calling
the style as a function, template literal tagging is not provided, and the
global level is process-wide rather than per-instance. Rarely supported
attributes such as [Style.Italic] and [Style.Overline] are included for
completeness even though not every terminal honors them.

types.name: Level
types.doc: Level is the color capability of the output terminal.

funcs.name: GetLevel
funcs.doc: GetLevel returns the current global color level.

types.name: Style
types.doc:
Style is an immutable, chainable set of terminal styles. Build one with New()
and the fluent methods, then render with Sprint/Sprintf/Print/Println.

funcs.name: New
funcs.doc: New returns an empty Style.

methods.name: Ansi256
methods.doc:
Ansi256 sets the foreground to a 256-palette color (0–255), degrading to 16
colors on limited terminals.

methods.name: BgAnsi256
methods.doc: BgAnsi256 sets the background to a 256-palette color (0–255).

methods.name: BgBlack
methods.doc: BgBlack sets a black background.

methods.name: BgBlue
methods.doc: BgBlue sets a blue background.

methods.name: BgBrightBlack
methods.doc: BgBrightBlack sets a bright black background.

methods.name: BgBrightBlue
methods.doc: BgBrightBlue sets a bright blue background.

methods.name: BgBrightCyan
methods.doc: BgBrightCyan sets a bright cyan background.

methods.name: BgBrightGreen
methods.doc: BgBrightGreen sets a bright green background.

methods.name: BgBrightMagenta
methods.doc: BgBrightMagenta sets a bright magenta background.

methods.name: BgBrightRed
methods.doc: BgBrightRed sets a bright red background.

methods.name: BgBrightWhite
methods.doc: BgBrightWhite sets a bright white background.

methods.name: BgBrightYellow
methods.doc: BgBrightYellow sets a bright yellow background.

methods.name: BgCyan
methods.doc: BgCyan sets a cyan background.

methods.name: BgGray
methods.doc: BgGray is an alias for BgBrightBlack.

methods.name: BgGreen
methods.doc: BgGreen sets a green background.

methods.name: BgHSL
methods.doc:
BgHSL sets the background from an HSL color: hue 0–360, saturation 0–100 and
lightness 0–100.

methods.name: BgHSV
methods.doc:
BgHSV sets the background from an HSV color: hue 0–360, saturation 0–100 and
value/brightness 0–100.

methods.name: BgHWB
methods.doc:
BgHWB sets the background from an HWB color: hue 0–360, whiteness 0–100 and
blackness 0–100.

methods.name: BgHex
methods.doc: BgHex sets the background from a hex color.

methods.name: BgMagenta
methods.doc: BgMagenta sets a magenta background.

methods.name: BgRGB
methods.doc: BgRGB sets the background to a 24-bit color.

methods.name: BgRed
methods.doc: BgRed sets a red background.

methods.name: BgWhite
methods.doc: BgWhite sets a white background.

methods.name: BgYellow
methods.doc: BgYellow sets a yellow background.

methods.name: Black
methods.doc: Black colors text black.

methods.name: Blue
methods.doc: Blue colors text blue.

methods.name: Bold
methods.doc: Bold makes text bold.

methods.name: BrightBlack
methods.doc: BrightBlack colors text bright black (gray).

methods.name: BrightBlue
methods.doc: BrightBlue colors text bright blue.

methods.name: BrightCyan
methods.doc: BrightCyan colors text bright cyan.

methods.name: BrightGreen
methods.doc: BrightGreen colors text bright green.

methods.name: BrightMagenta
methods.doc: BrightMagenta colors text bright magenta.

methods.name: BrightRed
methods.doc: BrightRed colors text bright red.

methods.name: BrightWhite
methods.doc: BrightWhite colors text bright white.

methods.name: BrightYellow
methods.doc: BrightYellow colors text bright yellow.

methods.name: Cyan
methods.doc: Cyan colors text cyan.

methods.name: Dim
methods.doc: Dim makes text dim/faint.

methods.name: Gray
methods.doc: Gray is an alias for BrightBlack.

methods.name: Green
methods.doc: Green colors text green.

methods.name: Grey
methods.doc: Grey is an alias for BrightBlack.

methods.name: HSL
methods.doc:
HSL sets the foreground from an HSL color: hue 0–360, saturation 0–100 and
lightness 0–100.

methods.name: HSV
methods.doc:
HSV sets the foreground from an HSV color: hue 0–360, saturation 0–100 and
value/brightness 0–100.

methods.name: HWB
methods.doc:
HWB sets the foreground from an HWB color: hue 0–360, whiteness 0–100 and
blackness 0–100.

methods.name: Hex
methods.doc: Hex sets the foreground from a hex color like "#ff8800" or "f80".

methods.name: Hidden
methods.doc: Hidden hides text.

methods.name: Inverse
methods.doc: Inverse swaps foreground and background.

methods.name: Italic
methods.doc: Italic makes text italic (not widely supported).

methods.name: Level
methods.doc:
Level pins this style to a specific color level regardless of the global
setting (useful for testing or forcing output).

methods.name: Magenta
methods.doc: Magenta colors text magenta.

methods.name: Overline
methods.doc: Overline draws a line above text.

methods.name: Print
methods.doc: Print writes the styled operands to stdout.

methods.name: Printf
methods.doc: Printf writes a styled formatted string to stdout.

methods.name: Println
methods.doc: Println writes the styled operands and a newline to stdout.

methods.name: RGB
methods.doc:
RGB sets the foreground to a 24-bit color, degrading to 256/16 colors as
needed.

methods.name: Red
methods.doc: Red colors text red.

methods.name: Reset
methods.doc: Reset clears all styles.

methods.name: Sprint
methods.doc: Sprint styles the concatenation of its operands (like fmt.Sprint).

methods.name: Sprintf
methods.doc: Sprintf styles a formatted string.

methods.name: Sprintln
methods.doc:
Sprintln styles its operands with a trailing newline (the newline is outside
the style codes).

methods.name: Strikethrough
methods.doc: Strikethrough draws a line through text.

methods.name: Underline
methods.doc: Underline underlines text.

methods.name: Visible
methods.doc:
Visible returns a style that emits its text only when color output is enabled
(the color level is greater than [LevelNone]). When color is disabled the
render methods return an empty string instead of the raw text. This mirrors
Node chalk's ".visible" modifier, handy for decorations that should vanish in
plain, piped output.

methods.name: White
methods.doc: White colors text white.

methods.name: Yellow
methods.doc: Yellow colors text yellow.

funcs.name: Ansi256
funcs.doc: Ansi256 colors text with a 256-palette index.

funcs.name: Ansi256ToAnsi16
funcs.doc:
Ansi256ToAnsi16 reduces an xterm 256-color palette index to the nearest basic
16-color SGR foreground code (30–37 or 90–97).

funcs.name: Ansi256ToRGB
funcs.signature: func Ansi256ToRGB(n int) (r, g, b int)
funcs.doc:
Ansi256ToRGB converts an xterm 256-color palette index (0–255) back to an
approximate RGB triple.

funcs.name: BgAnsi256
funcs.doc: BgAnsi256 styles text on a 256-palette background color.

funcs.name: BgBlack
funcs.doc: BgBlack styles text on a black background.

funcs.name: BgBlue
funcs.doc: BgBlue styles text on a blue background.

funcs.name: BgCyan
funcs.doc: BgCyan styles text on a cyan background.

funcs.name: BgGray
funcs.doc: BgGray styles text on a gray (bright black) background.

funcs.name: BgGreen
funcs.doc: BgGreen styles text on a green background.

funcs.name: BgHex
funcs.doc: BgHex styles text on a background color from a hex string like "#ff8800".

funcs.name: BgMagenta
funcs.doc: BgMagenta styles text on a magenta background.

funcs.name: BgRGB
funcs.signature: func BgRGB(r, g, b int, a ...any) string
funcs.doc: BgRGB styles text on a 24-bit background color.

funcs.name: BgRed
funcs.doc: BgRed styles text on a red background.

funcs.name: BgWhite
funcs.doc: BgWhite styles text on a white background.

funcs.name: BgYellow
funcs.doc: BgYellow styles text on a yellow background.

funcs.name: Black
funcs.doc: Black colors text black.

funcs.name: Blue
funcs.doc: Blue colors text blue.

funcs.name: Bold
funcs.doc: Bold styles text bold.

funcs.name: BrightBlack
funcs.doc: BrightBlack colors text bright black (gray).

funcs.name: BrightBlue
funcs.doc: BrightBlue colors text bright blue.

funcs.name: BrightCyan
funcs.doc: BrightCyan colors text bright cyan.

funcs.name: BrightGreen
funcs.doc: BrightGreen colors text bright green.

funcs.name: BrightMagenta
funcs.doc: BrightMagenta colors text bright magenta.

funcs.name: BrightRed
funcs.doc: BrightRed colors text bright red.

funcs.name: BrightWhite
funcs.doc: BrightWhite colors text bright white.

funcs.name: BrightYellow
funcs.doc: BrightYellow colors text bright yellow.

funcs.name: Cyan
funcs.doc: Cyan colors text cyan.

funcs.name: Dim
funcs.doc: Dim styles text dim.

funcs.name: Enabled
funcs.doc: Enabled reports whether any color will be emitted.

funcs.name: Gray
funcs.doc: Gray colors text gray (bright black).

funcs.name: Green
funcs.doc: Green colors text green.

funcs.name: HSL
funcs.signature: func HSL(h, sat, l int, a ...any) string
funcs.doc:
HSL styles text with an HSL foreground color (hue 0–360, saturation 0–100,
lightness 0–100).

funcs.name: HSLToRGB
funcs.signature: func HSLToRGB(h, s, l int) (r, g, b int)
funcs.doc:
HSLToRGB converts hue (0–360), saturation (0–100) and lightness (0–100) to an
RGB triple (0–255).

funcs.name: HSV
funcs.signature: func HSV(h, sat, v int, a ...any) string
funcs.doc:
HSV styles text with an HSV foreground color (hue 0–360, saturation 0–100,
value 0–100).

funcs.name: HSVToRGB
funcs.signature: func HSVToRGB(h, s, v int) (r, g, b int)
funcs.doc:
HSVToRGB converts hue (0–360), saturation (0–100) and value/brightness
(0–100) to an RGB triple (0–255).

funcs.name: HWB
funcs.signature: func HWB(h, w, bl int, a ...any) string
funcs.doc:
HWB styles text with an HWB foreground color (hue 0–360, whiteness 0–100,
blackness 0–100).

funcs.name: HWBToRGB
funcs.signature: func HWBToRGB(h, w, bl int) (r, g, b int)
funcs.doc:
HWBToRGB converts hue (0–360), whiteness (0–100) and blackness (0–100) to an
RGB triple (0–255).

funcs.name: Has256
funcs.doc:
Has256 reports whether the terminal supports the 256-color palette
([Level256] or higher). This is Node chalk's supportsColor.has256.

funcs.name: HasBasic
funcs.doc:
HasBasic reports whether the terminal supports at least the 16 basic ANSI
colors ([LevelBasic] or higher). This is Node chalk's supportsColor.hasBasic.

funcs.name: HasTrueColor
funcs.doc:
HasTrueColor reports whether the terminal supports 24-bit truecolor
([LevelTrueColor]). This is Node chalk's supportsColor.has16m.

funcs.name: Hex
funcs.doc: Hex colors text from a hex string like "#ff8800".

funcs.name: HexToRGB
funcs.signature: func HexToRGB(hex string) (r, g, b int)
funcs.doc:
HexToRGB parses a hex color such as "#ff8800", "ff8800" or the shorthand
"#f80" and returns its red, green and blue channels (each 0–255). Invalid
input yields (0, 0, 0).

funcs.name: Hidden
funcs.doc: Hidden styles text hidden (not displayed).

funcs.name: Inverse
funcs.doc: Inverse styles text inverted.

funcs.name: Italic
funcs.doc: Italic styles text italic.

funcs.name: Magenta
funcs.doc: Magenta colors text magenta.

funcs.name: Overline
funcs.doc: Overline styles text with an overline.

funcs.name: RGB
funcs.signature: func RGB(r, g, b int, a ...any) string
funcs.doc: RGB colors text with a 24-bit color.

funcs.name: RGBToAnsi16
funcs.signature: func RGBToAnsi16(r, g, b int) int
funcs.doc:
RGBToAnsi16 maps a 24-bit color to the nearest basic 16-color SGR foreground
code (30–37 for normal colors, 90–97 for the bright variants).

funcs.name: RGBToAnsi256
funcs.signature: func RGBToAnsi256(r, g, b int) int
funcs.doc:
RGBToAnsi256 maps a 24-bit color to the nearest xterm 256-color palette index
(0–255), using the same 6×6×6 color cube and grayscale ramp as color-convert.

funcs.name: RGBToHSL
funcs.signature: func RGBToHSL(r, g, b int) (h, s, l int)
funcs.doc:
RGBToHSL converts an RGB triple (0–255) to hue (0–360), saturation (0–100)
and lightness (0–100).

funcs.name: RGBToHSV
funcs.signature: func RGBToHSV(r, g, b int) (h, s, v int)
funcs.doc:
RGBToHSV converts an RGB triple (0–255) to hue (0–360), saturation (0–100)
and value/brightness (0–100).

funcs.name: RGBToHWB
funcs.signature: func RGBToHWB(r, g, b int) (h, w, bl int)
funcs.doc:
RGBToHWB converts an RGB triple (0–255) to hue (0–360), whiteness (0–100) and
blackness (0–100), the HWB color model used by CSS Color 4 and color-convert.

funcs.name: RGBToHex
funcs.signature: func RGBToHex(r, g, b int) string
funcs.doc:
RGBToHex formats an RGB triple as a lowercase "#rrggbb" hex string. Channels
are clamped to the 0–255 range first.

funcs.name: Red
funcs.doc: Red colors text red.

funcs.name: Reset
funcs.doc: Reset styles text with all attributes reset.

funcs.name: ResetDetection
funcs.doc: ResetDetection clears any forced level so the next use re-detects.

funcs.name: SetEnabled
funcs.doc: SetEnabled turns color on (auto-detected level, minimum basic) or off.

funcs.name: SetLevel
funcs.doc: SetLevel forces the global color level, overriding auto-detection.

funcs.name: Strikethrough
funcs.doc: Strikethrough styles text struck through.

funcs.name: Strip
funcs.doc: Strip removes all ANSI SGR escape sequences from s.

funcs.name: SupportsColor
funcs.doc:
SupportsColor reports whether any color will be emitted, i.e. the current
level is greater than [LevelNone]. It is an alias for [Enabled] provided for
parity with Node chalk's supportsColor.

funcs.name: Underline
funcs.doc: Underline styles text underlined.

funcs.name: Visible
funcs.doc:
Visible styles text so that it renders only when color output is enabled,
returning an empty string otherwise.

funcs.name: VisibleLength
funcs.doc:
VisibleLength returns the number of visible characters in s, ignoring ANSI
escape codes (counts runes, not bytes).

funcs.name: White
funcs.doc: White colors text white.

funcs.name: Yellow
funcs.doc: Yellow colors text yellow.

examples.name: Green
examples.doc:
ExampleGreen demonstrates the package-level shortcuts for one-off styling. It
forces the basic color level so the output is deterministic, then colors a
short string green using the Green shortcut, which is equivalent to
New().Green().Sprint. It also shows Strip, which removes every ANSI escape
sequence from a styled string, recovering the original plain text. Printing
both the quoted styled form and the stripped form side by side highlights the
difference between the on-the-wire bytes and what the user sees. The takeaway
is that shortcuts are a concise way to style a single value and that Strip
reverses the styling for measurement or logging.

examples.name: Style
examples.doc:
ExampleStyle demonstrates the fluent styling API end to end. It pins the
global color level to LevelBasic with SetLevel so the emitted ANSI escape
codes are deterministic regardless of the terminal running the test, then
resets detection afterward so other tests are unaffected. A single Style is
built by chaining Red and Bold and rendered with Sprint, which wraps the text
in the corresponding open/close SGR sequences. The result is printed with the
%q verb so the normally invisible escape bytes show up literally, making clear
that Red opens code 31 and Bold opens code 1, each closed in reverse order.
The takeaway is that a chained Style is immutable and produces predictable
escape sequences once the color level is fixed.

packages.name: figlet
packages.synopsis: Package figlet renders text as ASCII-art banners using FIGfont, a Go port of the classic figlet program and the Node figlet library.
packages.doc:
Package figlet renders text as ASCII-art banners using FIGfont, a Go port of
the classic figlet program and the Node figlet library. It ships a built-in
block font plus a large registry of bundled variants, and it can load any
standard .flf FIGfont from a file, directory or reader.

	fmt.Println(figlet.Render("Hi!"))                    // built-in font
	f, _ := figlet.LoadFontFile("slant.flf")
	fmt.Println(f.Render("Hello"))

Use figlet to draw large banner text for CLI splash screens, section headers
in logs, or generated README art. The simplest entry point is [Render], which
renders a string with the built-in font; [RenderFont] renders with a named
font from the registry (see [Fonts] and [GetFont]); and a [Font] value
obtained from [LoadFont], [LoadFontFile] or [ParseFont] can render directly
with [Font.Render]. The companion helpers [RenderRainbow] and [RenderGradient]
(and the lower-level [Rainbow] and [Gradient]) colorize a finished banner with
the sibling chalk package.

A FIGfont describes each printable character as a small block of text rows,
all the same height. Rendering lays the glyphs for the input left to right and
combines each pair of adjacent glyphs according to a [Layout]: full width
leaves them separate, kerning slides them together until they touch, and
smushing overlaps their touching edges and fuses the overlapping cells using
the font's "smushing rules". [LayoutDefault] honors whatever the font's header
specifies. Fonts may use a "hardblank" character that occupies space during
layout but prints as a blank, which is how figlet keeps letters from fusing
into an unreadable blob; hardblanks are replaced with spaces in the final
output.

Important semantics and edge cases: input is split on newlines and each line
is rendered as its own block, so multi-line input produces stacked banners.
Characters the font does not define fall back to the uppercase form (so fonts
that only define capitals still render mixed-case text) and finally to a
space, meaning unknown runes never abort a render. [ParseFont] validates the
flf2a signature and header and returns an error for a malformed font, but it
tolerates a truncated glyph table by rendering whatever characters it managed
to read. Output is plain text containing no ANSI codes unless you pass it
through one of the color helpers.

Parity with the original tools is partial and deliberate. The .flf format,
the four layout modes and the standard horizontal smushing rules (equal
character, underscore, hierarchy, opposite pair, big-X and hardblank) are all
implemented, so real-world fonts render faithfully. What is intentionally left
out is vertical smushing, right-to-left print direction, control files (.flc)
and character-code remapping. In exchange this port bundles its own fonts and
programmatically generates roughly a thousand named variants (see
fonts_generated.go) so useful output is available with zero external font
files.

types.name: Font

funcs.name: BuiltinFont
funcs.doc: BuiltinFont returns the built-in block font.

funcs.name: FontFromGlyphs
funcs.signature: func FontFromGlyphs(height, layout int, glyphs map[rune][]string) *Font
funcs.doc:
FontFromGlyphs builds a *Font from a glyph map of the given row height. Rows
within a glyph need not be equal width (they are padded at render time), but
every glyph must have exactly height rows. layout selects horizontal spacing:
use 0 for kerning (characters slide together until they touch, the natural
look for proportional fonts) or -1 for full fixed width.

Lowercase input automatically falls back to the uppercase glyph, so fonts
that define only capitals still render mixed-case text.

funcs.name: GetFont
funcs.doc: GetFont returns a registered font by name (case-insensitive).

funcs.name: LoadFont
funcs.doc: LoadFont parses a FIGfont from a reader.

funcs.name: LoadFontFile
funcs.doc: LoadFontFile parses a FIGfont from a .flf file on disk.

funcs.name: ParseFont
funcs.doc: ParseFont reads a FIGfont from r.

methods.name: Height
methods.doc: Height returns the number of rows in a rendered line.

methods.name: Render
methods.doc: Render renders text using the font.

types.name: Layout
types.doc: Layout selects how adjacent characters are combined horizontally.

types.name: Options

funcs.name: Fonts
funcs.doc: Fonts returns the sorted names of all registered fonts.

funcs.name: Gradient
funcs.signature: func Gradient(banner, startHex, endHex string) string
funcs.doc:
Gradient colors a rendered banner with a left-to-right color gradient from
startHex to endHex (e.g. "#ff0080" → "#00d7ff"). Each column is tinted by its
horizontal position.

funcs.name: LoadFontDir
funcs.doc:
LoadFontDir parses every .flf FIGfont in dir and registers each under its
base file name (without extension). It returns the names loaded.

funcs.name: Rainbow
funcs.doc: Rainbow colors a rendered banner across the full hue spectrum by column.

funcs.name: Register
funcs.doc:
Register adds a named font to the registry, making it available to
RenderFont and GetFont.

funcs.name: Render
funcs.doc: Render renders text with the built-in font.

funcs.name: RenderFont
funcs.signature: func RenderFont(name, text string, opts ...Options) (string, error)
funcs.doc: RenderFont renders text with a named registered font.

funcs.name: RenderGradient
funcs.signature: func RenderGradient(text, startHex, endHex string) string
funcs.doc: RenderGradient renders text with the built-in font and applies a gradient.

funcs.name: RenderRainbow
funcs.doc: RenderRainbow renders text with the built-in font and applies rainbow colors.

examples.name: Render
examples.doc:
ExampleRender demonstrates rendering a word as an ASCII-art banner with the
built-in block font. It calls Render on the two-character string "Hi", which
lays out the glyph for each letter side by side and joins their rows into a
five-line block. Because the built-in font is deterministic, the exact art is
known ahead of time and checked with an Output comment. Each rendered row is
right-trimmed before printing so that the font's internal padding spaces do
not appear as trailing whitespace, which keeps the expected output stable. The
takeaway is that Render turns ordinary text into multi-row banner art using
the bundled font with no font files or configuration required.

examples.name: RenderFont
examples.doc:
ExampleRenderFont demonstrates rendering with a named font drawn from the
registry rather than the default. It renders the word "Go" with the bundled
"banner" outline font by calling RenderFont, which looks the font up by
case-insensitive name and returns an error if the name is unknown. The number
of output rows equals the font's Height, so the code prints that height to
show the render succeeded without depending on the exact glyph art, which is
less stable across fonts. This also illustrates the two-value contract of
RenderFont: a rendered string plus an error that is nil on success. The
takeaway is that many named fonts are available through the registry via a
single call.

packages.name: prompts
packages.synopsis: Package prompts provides interactive terminal prompts in the spirit of Node's inquirer / @inquirer/prompts, styled with the sibling chalk package.
packages.doc:
Package prompts provides interactive terminal prompts in the spirit of Node's
inquirer / @inquirer/prompts, styled with the sibling chalk package. It offers
text input, password, confirm, number, single-select, and multi-select
prompts.

	name, _ := prompts.Input(prompts.InputConfig{Message: "Your name?"})
	ok, _ := prompts.Confirm(prompts.ConfirmConfig{Message: "Continue?", Default: true})
	i, choice, _ := prompts.Select(prompts.SelectConfig{
		Message: "Pick one",
		Choices: []prompts.Choice{{Name: "Red"}, {Name: "Green"}, {Name: "Blue"}},
	})

Use this package to ask a user questions from a command-line program: collect
a value ([Input]), read a secret without echoing it ([Password]), confirm a
yes/no decision ([Confirm]), read a bounded number ([Number]), or let the user
pick from a list with the arrow keys ([Select] and [MultiSelect]). Each prompt
is driven by a small config struct — [InputConfig], [ConfirmConfig] and the
rest — that carries the message, defaults, validation and the input/output
streams, so the API stays flat and there is no shared prompt object to
construct first.

Internally every prompt runs the same loop. It puts the terminal into raw mode
(via golang.org/x/term) so keystrokes arrive one at a time, decodes each
keypress — including arrow-key escape sequences — with an internal key reader,
updates its in-memory state, and repaints. The line prompts echo characters as
they are typed (or a mask rune, or nothing for a hidden password) and
re-prompt when a Validate function rejects the input. The list prompts redraw
their whole frame in place using ANSI cursor-movement codes, moving a pointer
and, for [MultiSelect], toggling checkboxes. On acceptance the prompt clears
its frame and prints a one-line summary of the answer.

Two semantics matter most. First, cancellation: pressing Ctrl-C or Esc makes a
prompt return [ErrCanceled], which callers should treat distinctly from a
normal answer. Second, input handling in non-interactive contexts: because the
key reader works on any io.Reader, raw mode is skipped automatically when the
input is not a real terminal. Setting the config's In field to something like
strings.NewReader lets you script a prompt for tests or piped input, where the
scripted bytes drive the same code path a live keyboard would, and reaching
end of input behaves like pressing Enter on the current buffer. An empty
submission falls back to the configured default, and [Number] additionally
enforces optional Min/Max bounds and integer-only mode.

Parity with inquirer is by feel rather than by API. The prompt types, the
green "?" prefix, the pointer and checkbox styling, default values, validation
with inline error messages and the answer summary all echo the Node
experience. The differences are that configuration is a Go struct instead of
an options object, prompts are called as ordinary functions returning
(value, error) instead of returning promises, and inquirer features such as
separators, paged/filtered lists, editors and a plugin architecture are not
included. Styling is fixed by this package rather than themeable.

vars.doc: ErrCanceled is returned when the user cancels a prompt (Ctrl-C or Esc).

types.name: Choice
types.doc: Choice is a selectable option for Select / MultiSelect.

funcs.name: MultiSelect
funcs.doc:
MultiSelect presents a checkbox list: arrows to move, space to toggle, enter
to confirm. It returns the selected indices and choices.

funcs.name: Select
funcs.doc:
Select presents a single-choice list navigated with the arrow keys. It
returns the selected index and choice.

types.name: ConfirmConfig
InputConfig
MultiSelectConfig
NumberConfig
PasswordConfig
SelectConfig

funcs.name: Confirm
funcs.doc: Confirm prompts for a yes/no answer, returning the boolean result.

funcs.name: Input
funcs.doc: Input prompts for a line of text.

funcs.name: Number
funcs.doc: Number prompts for a numeric answer with optional bounds.

funcs.name: Password
funcs.doc: Password prompts for a hidden (or masked) secret.

examples.name: Confirm
examples.doc:
ExampleConfirm demonstrates a scripted yes/no prompt. The config sets a
Default of false, and the scripted input "y\r" answers yes and submits, so
Confirm returns true. As with the other prompts, In is a strings.Reader and
Out is a throwaway buffer, so no real terminal is involved and the result is
fully deterministic. Confirm interprets "y" and "yes" (case-insensitively) as
true, "n" and "no" as false, and an empty line as the configured Default. The
takeaway is that Confirm reduces a line of input to a boolean while still
honoring a default for empty submissions.

examples.name: Input
examples.doc:
ExampleInput demonstrates driving a text prompt non-interactively. Instead of
reading from a real terminal, it points the config's In field at a
strings.Reader that supplies the scripted keystrokes "Ada" followed by a
carriage return, which stands in for the user typing a name and pressing
Enter. The rendered prompt is sent to a discarded bytes.Buffer via Out so the
example does not depend on terminal styling. Because the input is not a TTY,
the prompt skips raw mode and simply consumes the scripted bytes, returning
the typed value and a nil error. The takeaway is that any prompt can be tested
or scripted by supplying In and Out, exercising the exact code path a live
keyboard would.
